title: Colic and Gas in Babies: Gentle Soothing
excerpt: When a baby cries inconsolably after feeding, Ayurveda sees a small, immature digestive fire meeting an imbalance of Vata, and has a toolkit of gentle, time-tested remedies that parents have relied on for centuries.
body_html:
that relentless evening cry

Every parent knows the feeling. The baby has been fed, changed, and held, yet the crying continues, the little belly feels tight, and nothing seems to help. In most cases, what you are dealing with is what Ayurveda calls Shula (शूल), meaning colicky pain, or Adhmana (आध्मान), meaning distension and gas. Modern paediatrics has its own term for this: infant colic. But Ayurveda, particularly in the Kashyapa Samhita, the classical text devoted to children's health, offers a surprisingly detailed explanation of why babies suffer this way and what a parent can gently do about it.

The classical view is that a newborn's digestive fire, called Agni, is naturally weak (Kashyapa Samhita, Khilasthana). This is not a defect. It is simply the nature of a body that has just arrived in the world. A delicate Agni cannot always process every feed perfectly, and whatever is not digested fully produces Ama, an incompletely processed residue, which mixes with gases in the gut and causes the cramping and bloating you see in a colicky infant.

vata at the root

Ayurveda organises all bodily functions around three biological energies called Doshas: Vata, Pitta, and Kapha. In infants, Vata, the energy of movement and air, tends to be easily disturbed. The Ashtanga Hridaya describes Vata as the force responsible for all movement in the body, including the peristalsis that moves food through the gut (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 1). When Vata rises out of balance, movement becomes erratic. Gas builds, bowels cramp, and the child becomes restless and difficult to settle.

Several things can tip infant Vata out of balance: irregular feeding times, the mother consuming Vata-aggravating foods like raw vegetables, beans, or very cold drinks while breastfeeding, exposure to cold or dry air, or simply the natural accumulation of gas that happens as a baby learns to feed. Understanding this helps parents see that colic is not random. It usually follows a pattern, and Ayurvedic management works by gently calming that Vata and supporting the baby's immature Agni.

practical remedies, gentle and grounded

The most celebrated Ayurvedic remedy for infant gas is Hing, the resin known in English as asafoetida. Because giving any substance by mouth to a very young infant requires great caution, the classical approach is to apply a tiny pinch of Hing dissolved in warm water as a paste, dabbing it around the navel area externally. Parents across India have done this for generations, and the warming, carminative quality of Hing is thought to help disperse trapped gas without entering the baby's body directly. The Ashtanga Hridaya mentions Hing among the key carminative (Vata-pacifying) substances in its chapter on dietary substances (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6).

Warm oil massage is another cornerstone of Ayurvedic infant care. Sesame oil, slightly warmed, applied to the baby's abdomen with gentle clockwise strokes, follows the direction of natural gut movement and helps calm Vata. The Kashyapa Samhita recommends oil massage (Abhyanga) as a daily practice for infants, calling it nourishing to the skin, calming to the nervous system, and supportive of healthy growth (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana). Parents who make a short, gentle belly massage part of the post-bath routine often notice a real reduction in evening fussiness.

For breastfeeding mothers, diet matters deeply. The classical texts advise mothers to favour warm, freshly cooked, easily digestible foods. A small amount of Cumin (Jeera) and Fennel (Saunf) boiled in water and consumed by the nursing mother is traditionally recommended to reduce gassiness in both mother and infant, as these carminative seeds are understood to pass their beneficial qualities into the breast milk. The Ashtanga Hridaya lists both Cumin and Fennel as carminatives and digestive aids (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 6). Avoiding large quantities of raw salads, cruciferous vegetables, cold dairy, and beans during the early months of nursing is a simple step that many mothers find helpful.

when to see a doctor

The remedies above are supportive and gentle, but they are general information, not a substitute for medical care. Please consult a paediatrician or qualified Ayurvedic vaidya if your baby's crying is severe or does not settle with comfort measures, if the abdomen appears unusually hard or swollen, if your baby is not feeding well or is losing weight, if you notice blood in the stool, if there is vomiting that is forceful or green, or if your baby has a fever. These can be signs of conditions that need immediate medical evaluation. Colic is common and usually resolves on its own by around three to four months, but a professional opinion gives you the confidence that nothing more serious is being missed.

Try This Today

  • Hing paste on the navel: Dissolve a tiny pinch of Hing (asafoetida) in a few drops of warm water to form a thin paste. Apply gently around the baby's navel area externally. Do not give it by mouth to very young infants.
  • Warm sesame oil belly massage: Warm a small amount of sesame oil between your palms. Using gentle, clockwise circular strokes, massage the baby's abdomen for two to three minutes after bath time each day.
  • Cumin-fennel water for nursing mothers: Boil half a teaspoon each of Cumin seeds and Fennel seeds in two cups of water for five minutes. Strain and sip one cup, warm, twice a day while breastfeeding.
  • Keep feeds warm and timely: Try to feed at consistent intervals and ensure breast milk or formula is at a comfortably warm temperature, never cold. Cold feeds increase Vata and slow the infant's delicate digestion.
  • Bicycle leg movements: Gently hold the baby's ankles and move the legs in a slow cycling motion for one to two minutes. This mechanical movement helps shift trapped gas downward and is a natural complement to the oil massage.
  • When to call your doctor: These measures are supportive only. Consult a paediatrician or qualified vaidya promptly if your baby's crying is severe or inconsolable, the abdomen feels hard or swollen, the baby is not feeding or is losing weight, you see blood in the stool, vomiting is forceful or green-tinged, or the baby has a fever.

Key Ayurvedic Ingredients

Cumin (जीरा)

A warming carminative that helps dispel gas; nursing mothers are advised to drink cumin water to ease infant bloating

Fennel (सौंफ)

A gentle digestive seed that soothes gut spasm; paired with cumin in the classic nursing-mother infusion

metafields.dek: When a baby cries inconsolably after feeding, Ayurveda sees a small, immature digestive fire meeting an imbalance of Vata, and has a toolkit of gentle, time-tested remedies that parents have relied on for centuries.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Infant colic (Shula) is rooted in weak digestive fire and disturbed Vata, not a random upset
Hing paste around the navel and warm sesame oil belly massage are the classical first-line supports
What the nursing mother eats, especially warm cumin and fennel water, directly influences the baby's gas

glossary_terms.definition: Colicky pain; the classical Ayurvedic term for cramping, griping pain in the abdomen
Abdominal distension and bloating caused by accumulated gas
The digestive and metabolic fire that transforms food into nourishment; weak Agni leads to incomplete digestion
The residue of incompletely digested food; considered the root of many digestive complaints in Ayurveda
The biological energy of movement and air; when imbalanced, it causes gas, cramping, and restlessness
Medicated or plain oil massage; a daily Ayurvedic self-care practice recommended from infancy

sources: Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana (infant care and Abhyanga)
Kashyapa Samhita, Khilasthana (neonatal Agni and digestion)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 1 (Vata and its role in bodily movement)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana (carminative and digestive substances including Hing, Cumin, Fennel)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Shula" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Shula is defined as: Colicky pain; the classical Ayurvedic term for cramping, griping pain in the abdomen

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to hing paste on the navel. Dissolve a tiny pinch of Hing (asafoetida) in a few drops of warm water to form a thin paste. Apply gently around the baby's navel area externally. Do not give it by mouth to very young infants.

faqs.question: What is the benefit of Cumin for this condition?
faqs.answer: According to classical texts, Cumin is highly regarded. A warming carminative that helps dispel gas; nursing mothers are advised to drink cumin water to ease infant bloating
